content.full_text:
State of California

Memorandum

To : File                                          Date : February 25, 1982




From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
         Janis McLean

Subject: Reporting After Probate, but before an Order for
          Distribution
          Requesting Party: Anthony Alperin


          Question: Filer has a 10% or greater interest in
          an existing trust. Under the terms of a will, real
          property is to go into the trust. (The filer's pro
          rata share is in excess of $1,000). The will has been
          probated, but there is no Order for Distribution yet.
          Is the real property reportable on Schedule C, Form
          730?

          Answer: Disclosure is required where:

          1.    The filer is reasonably sure that the trust
                will receive the property (e.g., there is no
                will contest; the estate isn't so heavily in
                debt that it'likely the money will be taken
                for taxes, etc.).

          2.    The filer is reasonably sure that the amount/real
                property received by the trust will be over
                the threshold amount.

JM:plh

September 2, 1981


Marshall L. McIntyre
P.O. Box 5189
Hemet, CA 92343

Re: Form 721, Statement of
Economic Interests

Dear Mr. McIntyre:

Your incomplete Form 721 has been referred to this
office for our review. You have failed to report sources of
income to you as an appraiser. On Schedule H you have asserted
a privilege against disclosure based on the Code of Ethics
of three professional organizations.1/ In and of themselves,
these do not constitute "a legally recognized privilege
under California law."2/ Unless you have further legal
evidence to support your claim, I must advise you that the
privilege which you assert is nonexistent.

If you do have further legal evidence, then I would
suggest that you follow the procedures set forth in 2 Cal.
Adm. Code Section 18740. However I would also like to remind
you that sources of income to a business entity are only
reportable where your pro data share of the gross income
equals or exceeds $10,000 during the year.3/ However, you
would have to list your business as a source of income if

_________________________________

1/ The American Society of Appraisers, American Institute
of Real Estate Appraisers, and the Society of Real Estate
Appraisers.

2/ See our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18740,
copy enclosed.

3/ For a detailed analysis on this issue see the Commission's
Opinion No. 76-087, requested by Scott T. Carey, issued
Nov. 3, 1977, copy enclosed. Remember disqualification is
required where your pro rata share is $250 or more in the
twelve month period preceding the decision.

Al L. McIntyre
September 2, 1981
Page Two

you receive more than $250 from it during the year. Obviously,
if you are sole proprietor, then income to the business is
income to you and you must report anyone who paid you $10,000
or more during the year.4/

Sincerely,

Robert E. Delight
Counsel
Legal Division

REL:plh
GC: Edward J. Kodeghiet
Enclosures

_________________________________

4/    See Government Code Section 87207(b)(3), copy enclosed.

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 057 • SACRAMENTO, 95801 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance/Advice . . . Executive Legal . . . Enforcement/Complaint of Interest
(916) 322-5660 322-5761 322-6441

November 5, 1976

Edward B. Fechan, M.D.
625 West Olive Avenue
Merced, California 95340

Dear DR. Fechan:

Reference our telephone discussion of October 26, 1976, and your letter of October 11, 1976, to Governor Edmund G. Brown, Jr., requesting information pertaining to disclosure of income received from clients by persons serving on public boards.

I attach for your review a copy of the Political Reform Act of 1974. Although this not yet been amended at numerous audit hearings during the recent legislative session, for the purpose of your questions, the enclosed copy continue to be relevant. I direct your attention to Chapter 7, Article 2, of the Act (at page 42).

Part B of Form 700-A does contain a requirement for various affiliations and income. In most cases, those sources of income continue to be subject to your questions. Pursuant to the provisions of Article 2, those persons named in Section 87200, are required to file Statements of Economic Interests. The initial statements required do not include income (see Section 87202). The anniversary and leave office statements, described in Sections 87203 and 87204, do require income to be disclosed.

Senate Bill 1356, which became effective January 1, 1977, modifies the income reporting requirements of the above-referenced sections. Section 87203 (at page 15) amends this additional section does not require income to the jurisdiction. Senate Bill 1356, however, amends this Section to provide that income does not include income from any source outside the jurisdiction and not doing business within the jurisdiction, not planning to do business within the jurisdiction, or not having done business within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time any statement or other action is required under the Act. Accordingly, the income now required to be disclosed, pursuant to Sections 87203 and 87204 of Article 2, is limited to income from within the jurisdiction.

I now direct your attention to Chapter 7, Article 3 (at page 45) of the Act. Many persons serving on public bodies will be

[illegible]

Dr. Edward B. Pechan                    - 2 -                    November 5, 1976


required to disclose income, not because of Article 2 disclosure
requirements, but pursuant to a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by the agency that they serve. Section 87302(b) of
Article 3, makes income reportable if the income or source of
income may result in a conflict with the duties of a filer if
made or participated in by the designated employee by virtue
of his or her position.

Thus, persons serving on public bodies will be required to
disclose income either: (a) because they are public officials
subject to the disclosure provisions of Article 2 which require
income reporting, or (b) because they are designated employees
subject to the disclosure provisions of a Conflict of Interest Code
adopted by their agency, pursuant to the provisions of Article 3.
In either case, income disclosure is required of them.

I now direct your attention to Section 87207 which deals with
Disclosure of Income. This Section provides that when income is
required to be reported (under Article 2 or under Article 3) the
statement shall contain specified information. The answer to the
specific question that you address (concerning clients) is to be
found in this Section. Section 87307(b) provides that:

     "...When income of a business entity, including income of a
     sole proprietorship, is required to be reported under
     this article, the statement shall contain:

     (1) The name, address, and a general description of
     the business activity of the business entity;

     (2) In the case of a business entity which provides
     legal or brokerage services, the name of every person
     who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's
     pro rata share of fees from such person was equal to or
     greater than $1,000;

     (3) In the case of a business entity not covered by
     paragraph (2), the name of every person from whom the
     business entity received payments if the filer's pro
     rata share of gross receipts from such person was equal
     to or greater than $10,000 during a calendar year.

Basically, whenever a person owns, directly, indirectly
or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater, in a business
entity, the individual is required to disclose his or her pro
rata share of any income of the business entity. This requirement
is found in Section 32030 which defines income. Thus, if one owns

Dr. Leonard B. Peskin                    - 3 -                    November 5, 1970


15 percent of a brokerage firm, and the disclosure category under
which income is required to be disclosed involves income from
that type source, the provisions of Section 97207(b)(2) would
require disclosure of the name of every person who paid fees to
the business entity if the filer's pro rata share of fees was equal
to or greater than $1,000. This is a very complicated reporting
requirement. To understand it, you must recognize that there are
a number of conditions which must be present before disclosure
of the client's name would be required. First, if the person is
disclosing under Article 2 (high-ranking state or local public
officials) then documents would have to be met. First, the
filer would have to own a ten percent or greater interest in the
business entity; secondly, if the business entity provides legal
or brokerage services, the filer's pro rata share of the fees from
the person would have to equal or be greater than $1,000; thirdly,
the source (the client) of the income would have to be within the
jurisdiction or doing business within the jurisdiction or within
to business within the jurisdiction or have done business
within the jurisdiction during the two years prior to the time
the statement was required. It is a business entity which is
not a legal or brokerage firm, the same conditions as outlined
above are required except that the trigger amount is not $1,000, but
$10,000.

   Similarly, if the filer is disclosing as a designated employee
(not a public official and controlled in Article 2) a list of
sources Code defined in Item 2 or other items disclosing require-
ments must be met before disclosure of the name of the client is
required:

   First, pursuant to the provisions of Government Code Section
97302(b), the income can be made reportable only if it is fore-
seeable that the income or the source of income may be affected
materially by any decision made or participated in by the designated
employee by virtue of his position. If the income does not fall
within the disclosure category, it is simply not reportable.
   Secondly, the individual must own, directly, indirectly or non-
partially, a ten percent interest or greater in the business entity.
Thirdly, the filer's pro rata share of fees paid to the business
entity from the individual client must be equal to or greater than
$1,000 if it is a legal or brokerage firm or the pro rata share
must be equal to or greater than $10,000 from the individual
client if it is a non-legal or brokerage firm. If these conditions
are met, then the identity of the individual client from whom the
income is received must be disclosed.

   In a recent case, Dave v. Todd, involving the Commission,
disclosure of the name of an individual client was challenged.

Dr. Edward B. Feehan                    - 4 -                    November 5, 1976


The court ruled that the disclosure requirement does not violate
the attorney-client privilege, the right of either an attorney or his of her clients to the equal protection of the
laws by reason of the $1,000 reporting threshold for attorney
and brokers versus the $10,000 threshold for other persons.
Pursuant to that litigation, however, and in recognition that
there is a limited privilege from disclosure which flows from the
attorney-client relationship, the Commission has adopted a new
regulation which is to be found at 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section
18740; I attach a copy for your review. Basically, this regulation
permits an official to request exemption from this reporting
requirement and requires the applicant for exemption, to assert the
legal basis for the privilege and, as specifically as possible,
facts which demonstrate why the privilege is applicable to her or
her case. The Commission then determines whether or not the
request for exemption should be granted. The same limited restriction of a confidential relationship is applicable to physicians
and patients and to husband clients other than attorneys and
doctors.

That basically lays out the disclosure requirements pertaining
to the identity of an institutional client.

In addition, we ask about retroactive reporting requirements.
The initial statements filed, whether pursuant to Article 2 of the
Act or pursuant to a conflict of Interest Code adopted by the
agency, pursuant to Article 3, require only disclosure of investments, interests in real property, and, at the option of the
entity, management positions. Income is not required to be
disclosed during the initial reporting period. Subsequently,
however, whether on an annual statement or a leaving office statement, income is required to be reported.

I hope that this information will be of some help to you in
answering your personal questions and in permitting you to speak
on the subject should another speaking engagement be scheduled. I
attach for your review a copy of the Act and the relevant regulations. Please let me know if I can be of further assistance to you.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Edgar A. Kerry
                                        Staff Attorney
                                        Conflicts of Interest Division
                                        (916) 322-6444
EAK:mra

Enclosure

sections.question:
Filer has a 10% or greater interest in an existing trust. Under the terms of a will, real property is to go into the trust. (The filer's pro rata share is in excess of $1,000). The will has been probated, but there is no Order for Distribution yet. Is the real property reportable on Schedule C, Form 730?

Answer: Disclosure is required where:

1. The filer is reasonably sure that the trust will receive the property (e.g., there is no will contest; the estate isn't so heavily in debt that it's likely the money will be taken for taxes, etc.).

2. The filer is reasonably sure that the amount/real property received by the trust will be over the threshold amount.

JM:plh

September 2, 1981

Marshall L. McIntyre  
P.O. Box 5189  
Hemet, CA 92343

Re: Form 721, Statement of Economic Interests

Dear Mr. McIntyre:

Your incomplete Form 721 has been referred to this office for our review. You have failed to report sources of income to you as an appraiser. On Schedule H you have asserted a privilege against disclosure based on the Code of Ethics of three professional organizations.1/ In and of themselves, these do not constitute "a legally recognized privilege under California law."2/ Unless you have further legal evidence to support your claim, I must advise you that the privilege which you assert is nonexistent.

If you do have further legal evidence, then I would suggest that you follow the procedures set forth in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18740. However I would also like to remind you that sources of income to a business entity are only reportable where your pro rata share of the gross income equals or exceeds $10,000 during the year.3/ However, you would have to list your business as a source of income if

1/ The American Society of Appraisers, American Institute of Real Estate Appraisers, and the Society of Real Estate Appraisers.

2/ See our regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18740, copy enclosed.

3/ For a detailed analysis on this issue see the Commission's Opinion No. 76-087, requested by Scott T. Carey, issued Nov. 3, 1977, copy enclosed. Remember disqualification is required where your pro rata share is $250 or more in the twelve month period preceding the decision.

Al L. McIntyre  
November 2, 1981  
Page Twp

you receive more than $250 from it during the year. Obviously, if you are sole proprietor, then income to the business is income to you and you must report anyone who paid you $10,000 or more during the year.
